question.text: Exchange Traded Funds (ETFs) differ from regular index mutual funds primarily because:

answer.text: ETFs actively manage their portfolio while index funds follow passive strategy
ETFs can be bought and sold at real-time prices on stock exchanges while index funds transact at end-of-day NAV
ETFs have higher expense ratios than index funds
ETFs cannot be held in demat form

explanation.text: **The Logic:** ETF units are listed and traded in demat form on stock exchanges with prices changing continuously to reflect changes in the underlying index. Index funds transact at end-of-day NAV. ETFs typically have lower expense ratios due to passive management. **The Trap:** Option A reverses the reality — both ETFs and index funds are passively managed. Option C is wrong — ETFs typically have LOWER expense ratios. Option D is wrong — ETFs are held in demat form. **Cross-Exam Context:** ETFs are covered in NISM Series V-A, XV, and VIII. The creation/redemption mechanism through Authorized Participants is a key differentiator. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** ETFs combine the diversification of index funds with the trading flexibility of stocks. However, liquidity in Indian ETFs can vary — always check the bid-ask spread before recommending.
